question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GraphQL API does not implement connection pool warming after a network partition so that, when the partition heals, the first batch of findByEvmAddress() requests experiences high latency as HikariCP establishes new connections, causing a thundering-herd effect that overwhelms the recovering database?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GraphQL API does not implement a health check that specifically tests the findByAlias() query path so that, during a network partition, the /actuator/health endpoint may report healthy while the actual entity lookup functionality is completely broken, delaying operator response?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GraphQL API does not implement request prioritization so that, during a network partition recovery, low-priority attacker requests compete equally with high-priority legitimate requests for the limited available database connections, preventing fair recovery?
